communityVoices.quote: FUCK BARTON HALL for once and all - nobody listening can tell why the crescendoss are the key to Barton's adulation
The Half Step really swings here- one of my top 3 for '77 (and all time, really). The NFA clocks in at just over 20 minutes. Talk about grindin' it...down and dirty Jerry goes on for days here.
communityVoices.attribution: PFflyerkid, on Mississippi Half-Step and NFA

communityVoices.quote: This has always made my list of the five overrated shows. Like the others, not because it's not good, but because it's not, overall, a top ten of all time (or the year).
9/3/77 is not over nor under rated. It deserves to be ranked alongside other huge festivals played (Watkins Glen) not put up against small intimate venues (Cornell)
by sunset when the DEAD hit their stride I was just peaking and just remember the chorus of Trucking going on and on for like eternity!!!!!

whyThisShow: Englishtown on September 3, 1977 drew an estimated 107,000 to 150,000 people to a dirt field at Old Bridge Raceway Park in blistering late-summer heat, and the Dead delivered a show that has been in heavy circulation ever since [source: 'set the record for most tickets sold at a NJ concert']. One longtime listener framed it as a bridge show: "This is the bridge between that time and the often overlooked 78-79 Dead" [source: 'This is the bridge between that time and the often overlooked 78-79 Dead']. A dissenting voice worth knowing about is Mind Wondrin, who argues the show has earned an "overrated" tag more than any other, crediting its reputation partly to how widely it circulated as an early tape for many fans [source: 'This show probably gets the "overrated" tag more than any other, and Jarnow noted that he never really got this show, since it was "too big & shouty & not jamming"']. That said, it was broadcast on powerhouse WNEW-FM NYC that Labor Day weekend, putting it directly in the ears of a huge regional audience [source: 'this was broadcast on powerhouse WNEW-FM NYC that Labor Day weekend']. The show remains unreleased as a standalone title, though Dick's Picks 15 draws from it and covers most of the show with some edits [source: 'Dick’s 15 has the whole show save a few tunings and an edited version of He’s Gone'].
listenFor: Mississippi Half-Step is a consistent highlight; one reviewer ranked it among their top three Half-Steps of all of 1977 [source: 'The Half Step really swings here- one of my top 3 for \'77 (and all time, really)']. Eyes of the World is worth the wait in the second set, even if the Estimated before it is wobbly [source: 'Don\'t worry about the wobbly Estimated because Eyes of the World is worth the wait']. The NFA runs just over twenty minutes and one listener described Jerry going "down and dirty" for days [source: 'The NFA clocks in at just over 20 minutes. Talk about grindin\' it...down and dirty Jerry goes on for days here']. Mind Wondrin flags He's Gone as having flubs but notes the jam into NFA is the show's best moment [source: 'the jam into NFA is the show\'s best']. The SBD source circulating as unk_276 is the soundboard, though FM tapes also still circulate and a remastered SBD exists [source: 'The unk_276 is the SBD, but surely the FM tapes still circulate (as does a remastered SBD)'].
context: This was the first show in three months after the summer tour was cancelled when Mickey Hart was injured rolling his Porsche, making it a one-off booking before the fall tour [source: 'This was the 1st show in 3mos, after the summer tour was cancelled when Mickey was injured rolling his Porsche']. It was also the first gig where Keith only brought electric-based keys, and Jerry was playing his Travis Bean TB500 modded with two outputs [source: 'This was the 1st gig where Keith only brought electric-based keys. Jer played his Travis Bean TB500, now modded with two outputs']. Donna had surgery during the summer and only decided at the last minute to appear for some songs [source: 'Donna had a surgery and only decided at the last minute to appear for some songs']. The best circulating soundboard is archived at gd77-09-03.sbd.unk.276.sbefixed.shnf; the show has never received a standalone official release.
funFact: John Scher hired stevedores to bring hundreds of shipping containers and encircle the entire area end-to-end so there were no gatecrashers [source: 'John Scher went to Amboy and hired stevedores to bring hundreds of shipping containers and encircle the area end-to-end so there were no gatecrashers'].
headline: 150,000 people, one scorching day at the drag strip.
